CROSS-DEFENDANT NICKESH SHAH, M.D.'s DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT - Cross-defendant Nickesh Shah's demurrer to cross-complainants Lawrence and Pamela Stephens' second amended cross-complaint is: a) overruled as to the eleventh cause of action for intentional interference with contractual relations and the fourteenth cause of action (incorrectly labeled as the thirteenth in SACC) for violation of the Unfair Competition Law and b) sustained without leave to amend as to the twelfth and thirteenth causes of action for intentional and negligent interference with economic relations and the fifteenth cause of action (incorrectly labeled as the fourteenth in the SACC) for financial elder abuse. The Stephens adequately allege claims for intentional interference with contractual relations and violation of the UCL based on the unfair and fraud prongs. The Stephens adequately allege that Dr. Shah's conduct induced interference with their contracts with Kulwantbir Sandhu and their landlord since Dr. Shah's actions led to Mr. Sandhu's inability to pay rent or make payments pursuant to the stock purchase agreement. Liberally construed, the Stephens' UCL claim adequately alleges that Dr. Shah committed unfair and misleading conduct to the detriment of AMMC's customers which is sufficient to state a claim for violation of the UCL on the unfair and fraud prongs. The Stephens do not allege independent wrongful conduct by Dr. Shah which is a required element to state a claim for both intentional and negligent interference with prospective economic relations. (See National Medical Transp. Network v. Deloitte & Touche (1998) 62 Cal.App.4th 412, 440.) Mr. Stephens fails to adequately allege the first element of CACI 3100 of misappropriating property for a financial elder abuse claim per Welfare & Institutions Code 15610. 30. The SACC alleges that Mr. Stephens only retained a security interest in Advanced Male Medical Center. Despite the broad definition of property and the transferability of security interests, Mr. Stephens sold his interests in AMMC to Mr. Sandhu subject to the stock purchase agreement and no longer had the right to control or possess the property alleged to have been taken. The Stephenses are not granted leave to amend as to their deficient economic advantage and financial elder abuse claims because they have already had three opportunities to allege cognizable claims, the trial is 70 days away, and they have not shown that it is reasonably possible for them to cure the deficiencies in these claims. Order signed in open court. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Carol Karen CSR #8189

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-24-2017 Sustained
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Tad A. Devlin of the law offices of Kaufman Dolowich & Voluck LLP (415.926.7635) appears as counsel for plaintiff/cross-complainent/opposing party,

Attorney, Rex Grady of the law offices of SmithDollar PC (707.522.1100 ext. 1113) appears as counsel for cross-defendant/moving party, Nickesh Shah

No other appearance is made in this matter

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter. The Court confirms in part and reverses in part the tentative ruling.

CROSS DEFENDANT NICKESH SHAH, M.D. DEMURRER TO FIRST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT filed by Lawrence Stephens and Pamela Stephens is SUSTAINED with twenty days leave to amend as to the eleventh cause of action for conspiracy, the twelfth cause of action for violation of the Unfair Competition Law and the thirteenth cause of action for financial elder abuse. The eleventh cause of action is a hodge-podge of alleged misconduct with an arguably inapt label of conspiracy. A party to a contract or someone with whom there is a prospective economic advantage such as Mr. Sandhu is alleged to be with regards to the Stephens cannot conspire to interfere with his own contract or advantage. By including allegations that Dr. Shah conspired with Mr. Sandhu to interfere with their contract and economic advantage with Mr. Sandhu, the Stephens have made their eleventh cause of action uncertain. If they can do so in good faith, the Stephens are given leave to allege one or more claims alleging that Dr. Shah directly committed an interference tort (CACI 2200 series) and/or that he conspired with Dr. Peterson to commit an interference tort. Because the Stephens' UCL claim against Dr. Shah is at least partially dependent on the allegations in the eleventh cause of action, it too is uncertain and, if they can do so in good faith, the Stephens are given leave to amend to clearly allege an unfair, illegal or fraudulent business practice allegedly committed by Dr. Shah. The demurrer is sustained as to the thirteenth cause of action because there
is insufficient allegations as to the first element of a financial abuse claim as set forth in CACI 3100. The prevailing party shall submit a [proposed] written order that tracks the Court's ruling, verbatim and that complies with CRC 3.1312.
